title: Maharashtrian Misal Pav

ingredients.name: moth beans or whole moong, sprouted (see notes)
visual_checks: soak overnight, drain and keep covered for 12–18 hrs until 1 cm tails appear
ingredients.notes: soak overnight, drain and keep covered for 12–18 hrs until 1 cm tails appear

subs.name: 2 cups whole green moong (soaked overnight and sprouted); or canned chickpeas drained well — reduces prep time to zero

ingredients.name: onion, finely chopped (2 for curry + 1 for garnish)

subs.name: 2 tbsp coconut milk powder dissolved in 3 tbsp water; add at the same stage

ingredients.name: tamarind pulp
visual_checks: gives the characteristic sour note of Kolhapuri-style misal
ingredients.notes: gives the characteristic sour note of Kolhapuri-style misal

subs.name: 1.5 tbsp lemon juice or 1 tbsp kokum extract

ingredients.name: sev or farsan (crispy chickpea snack mix), for topping
visual_checks: store-bought sev or mixed farsan; adds the essential crunch contrast
ingredients.notes: store-bought sev or mixed farsan; adds the essential crunch contrast

steps.text: Pressure-cook the sprouted beans with 1.5 cups water, turmeric and salt for 2 whistles on medium, then allow natural pressure release. The beans should be fully cooked but still holding their shape — not mushy. If using a pot, simmer covered for 20–25 minutes.

Grind together the grated coconut with 2 chopped garlic cloves and 2 tbsp of the cooked beans (for body) into a smooth paste using a mixie with very little water. This coconut masala paste is what distinguishes Maharashtrian misal from a plain dal.

Heat oil in a kadai on medium-high. Add mustard seeds and let them pop. Add curry leaves, remaining garlic, ginger and 2 chopped onions; sauté until deep golden, about 8 minutes. Add green chillies and tomatoes; cook until soft and oil surfaces, about 5 minutes more.

Add red chilli powder, coriander powder and garam masala; stir and fry for 30 seconds. Add the ground coconut paste and fry, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until the rawness disappears. Add the cooked sprouts along with all their cooking liquid.

Stir in tamarind pulp, jaggery and salt. Add 0.5 cup water if the usal looks too thick — misal gravy should be pourable. Simmer on medium for 8 minutes, taste and adjust heat (add more red chilli powder if you want it hotter). The colour should be deep red-orange.

To assemble: ladle the hot usal into deep bowls. Top with a generous handful of sev or farsan, diced raw onion and coriander. Squeeze half a lemon over the top. Serve immediately with toasted pav on the side — the sev must be added at the last second to stay crunchy.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: sprouts → 2 cups whole green moong (soaked overnight and sprouted); or canned chickpeas drained well — reduces prep time to zero; tamarind → 1.5 tbsp lemon juice or 1 tbsp kokum extract; coconut → 2 tbsp coconut milk powder dissolved in 3 tbsp water; add at the same stage
